section.title: The Church at Antioch
section.sectionRationale: The believers scattered by Stephen's persecution reach Antioch, and some of them, men from Cyprus and Cyrene, take the daring step of preaching the Lord Jesus to Greeks as well as Jews. A great number believe, and a new kind of church is born, made of Jew and Gentile together. Jerusalem sends Barnabas, who rejoices, fetches Saul, and teaches for a year, and here the disciples are first called Christians. When famine looms, this young church sends relief back to Judea, showing a family bound across every barrier.
section.thesis: At Antioch the gospel deliberately crosses to the Greeks and creates a thriving mixed church of Jew and Gentile, encouraged by Barnabas and taught by Saul, so that a new missionary base is formed, the name Christian is first given, and the grace received flows out in generosity to others.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the gospel preached to the Greeks at Antioch and many believing; Barnabas sent and rejoicing at the grace of God; Barnabas fetching Saul, a year of teaching, and the name Christian; and the church sending famine relief to Judea.

units.label: The gospel reaches the Greeks at Antioch
units.note: a great number believe and turn to the Lord

verses.text: Meanwhile those scattered by the persecution that began with Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, speaking the message only to Jews.
But some of them, men from Cyprus and Cyrene, went to Antioch and began speaking to the {{hellen|Greeks}} as well, proclaiming the good news about the Lord Jesus.
The hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number of people believed and {{epistrepho|turned}} to the Lord.

units.label: Barnabas sent to Antioch
units.note: he saw the grace of God and rejoiced

verses.text: When news of this reached the ears of the church in Jerusalem, they sent Barnabas to Antioch.
When he arrived and saw the {{charis|grace}} of God, he rejoiced and encouraged them all to abide in the Lord with all their hearts.
Barnabas was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a great number of people were brought to the Lord.

units.label: Barnabas fetches Saul, and disciples are called Christians
units.note: a year of teaching in a mixed church

verses.text: Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul,

and when he found him, he brought him back to Antioch. So for a full year they met together with the church and taught large numbers of people. The disciples were first called {{christianos|Christians}} at Antioch.

units.label: Famine relief for Judea
units.note: grace received flows out in generosity

verses.text: In those days some prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch.
One of them named Agabus stood up and predicted through the Spirit that a great famine would sweep across the whole world. (This happened under Claudius.)
So the disciples, each according to his ability, decided to send relief to the brothers living in Judea.
This they did, sending their gifts to the elders with Barnabas and Saul.

hellen.gloss: A Greek, and more broadly a Gentile by culture, a non-Jew. That the scattered believers began preaching the Lord Jesus to Greeks as well as Jews marks a deliberate step across the old divide and the birth of a mixed church at Antioch.

epistrepho.gloss: To turn around, to turn back, to convert. The great number who turned to the Lord describes real conversion, a decisive turning from idols and self to the living Christ, the fruit of the hand of the Lord being with the preachers.

charis.gloss: Grace, the undeserved favor and kindness of God. Barnabas saw the grace of God, not merely human enthusiasm, in the changed lives at Antioch, and it was this evidence of grace that made him rejoice and call them to hold fast to the Lord.

christianos.gloss: Christian, one belonging to or identified with Christ. First given at Antioch, likely by outsiders, the name captures what the church is: a people so marked by Christ that His name becomes their name.

basic.context: The believers scattered by the persecution after Stephen carry the gospel north to Antioch, a great mixed city. At first they speak only to Jews, but some bolder men begin telling Greeks about the Lord Jesus too, and many believe. Jerusalem sends Barnabas to check on it; he sees God's grace at work, rejoices, and encourages them. He then fetches Saul from Tarsus, and together they teach the church for a year. Here the disciples are first called Christians. When a famine is predicted, the church sends relief to Judea. The key questions are: why is preaching to the Greeks so significant, what does Barnabas model, and why does grace lead to generosity.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the next stage after the Cornelius breakthrough: what God did once at Caesarea now becomes an ongoing, deliberate mission to Gentiles, producing a whole church. Watch the pattern of grace: God's hand brings people to faith, a mature encourager nurtures them, sound teaching establishes them, and the fruit is a new identity and outward-flowing love. The naming Christian and the famine gift are signs of a healthy, maturing church.
basic.originalLanguages: The daring move is to preach to Hellen, Greeks, and not Jews only. A great number epistrepho, turn, to the Lord in genuine conversion. Barnabas sees the charis, the grace, of God among them and rejoices. And the disciples are first called Christianos, Christians, those belonging to Christ. The words trace a mixed church born of grace and marked by Christ's own name.
basic.typology: Antioch begins to reverse the scattering of the nations, gathering Jew and Greek into one worshipping community. The name Christian anticipates the new humanity in Christ that Paul will describe. The famine offering foreshadows the pattern of grace producing generosity that runs through the New Testament collections.
basic.christ: The message is simply the Lord Jesus, and it is the hand of the Lord that makes it fruitful. The church is defined by Christ so fully that His people take His name. Everything good here, the conversions, the grace, the teaching, the generosity, flows from union with the risen Lord who is building His church among the nations.
basic.god: God's grace is the engine of the whole account. His hand is with the preachers, His grace is visible in changed lives, and His Spirit speaks through the prophet Agabus. Barnabas, full of the Spirit and faith, is God's gift to steady the young church. The story shows God building His people and providing for them through one another.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 13 shows Antioch becoming the launch point of Paul's missionary journeys. Acts 4 introduces Barnabas as the son of encouragement. Galatians 2 recalls Antioch's importance and its tensions. 2 Corinthians 8 and 9 develop the grace that overflows in generous giving.
basic.oneStory: Antioch is where the church of the nations comes of age. A mixed people, first called Christians, becomes a base from which the gospel will go to the ends of the earth. The grace they received turns outward in love, and the whole movement of Acts, from Jerusalem to the world, gathers momentum here.

context.literary: This passage rounds off the section that began with Stephen's death by showing where the scattering finally led: not to the death of the church but to Antioch and a new mission. It bridges the Cornelius account and the missionary journeys, introducing Antioch as the story's new centre of gravity and reuniting Barnabas with Saul, whom he had earlier vouched for in Jerusalem.
context.historical: Antioch on the Orontes was the third-largest city of the empire, a diverse commercial hub with a large Jewish community and countless Gentiles. Preaching the Lord Jesus to Greeks there was a natural but momentous step. The famine under Claudius is attested in the mid first century, and the relief sent to Judea reflects the poverty of the Jerusalem church.
context.canonical: Antioch becomes the second great centre of Christianity after Jerusalem and the springboard of the Gentile mission that fills the rest of Acts and Paul's letters. The name Christian, first coined here, becomes the enduring label of Christ's people. The famine collection anticipates the wider ministry of relief that marks the apostolic churches.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke shows how the scattering became mission, how a deliberate outreach to Greeks produced a thriving mixed church, and how mature encouragement and sound teaching, under God's grace, established a new base for the worldwide spread of the gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific events, the founding of the Antioch church, the coining of the name Christian, the particular famine gift, describe a decisive moment in history. What is prescriptive and enduring is the call to preach Christ across cultural lines, to encourage and teach new believers, and to let grace received overflow in generosity to others.

debates.question: To whom did the men from Cyprus and Cyrene preach, Greek-speaking Jews or Gentile Greeks?

views.label: Gentile Greeks, marking a new step beyond the Jews
views.case: The strongest case reads the Greeks as Gentiles, since Luke contrasts them in verse 20 with the Jews spoken to in verse 19, and the whole point is that the scattered believers now went further than before. The astonishment, the sending of Barnabas from Jerusalem, and the new mixed church that results all fit a deliberate turn to Gentiles, building on the Cornelius breakthrough.

views.label: Greek-speaking Jews or God-fearers only
views.case: A minority reads the word as Hellenists, Greek-speaking Jews, partly on a textual variant, which would make this less of a new departure. This respects a real manuscript question, but it blunts Luke's clear contrast with verse 19 and the significance he plainly attaches to what happened at Antioch.

debates.bearing: Taking the Greeks as Gentiles fits Luke's storyline that the gospel is now crossing to the nations in an ongoing way, and explains why Antioch becomes the mother church of the Gentile mission.

study.originalLanguages: The decisive words come quickly. Some believers begin preaching to Hellen, Greeks, not Jews only, and a great number epistrepho, turn, to the Lord in real conversion. Barnabas arrives and sees the charis, the grace, of God, the sure evidence that this is God's work and not mere excitement, and he rejoices. And the disciples are first called Christianos, Christians, a people so defined by Christ that they bear His name. Grace has made a new community among the nations.

typology.type: The gathering of Jew and Greek at Antioch
typology.fulfillment: A first flowering of the one new humanity in Christ, reversing the scattering of the nations and prefiguring the church drawn from every people.
The identity of those belonging to Christ, anticipating the New Testament vision of a people who bear His name and reflect His character.
The pattern of grace overflowing in generosity, fulfilled again in the apostolic collections and the church's care for the poor.

study.christ: The whole passage centres on the Lord Jesus. He is the message the believers preach, His hand makes the word fruitful, and His name becomes the church's name. The conversions, the grace Barnabas sees, the year of teaching, and the generosity toward Judea all flow from union with the risen Christ, who is building a people for Himself among Jew and Gentile alike and sending them out into the world.
study.god: God's grace drives everything. His hand is with the preachers so that many turn, His grace is visibly at work in changed lives, and His Spirit warns of famine through Agabus so His people can prepare and share. He raises up Barnabas, a good man full of the Spirit and faith, and reunites him with Saul to teach the church. The health and mission of Antioch are God's own gift.

crossReferences.note: The Antioch church, now established, becomes the base that sends out Barnabas and Saul on the first missionary journey.
Barnabas, the son of encouragement, introduced earlier, now lives up to his name at Antioch.
Antioch's later table-fellowship conflict shows both its importance and the ongoing struggle over Jew and Gentile unity.
The grace of God producing generous giving, the same movement seen in Antioch's famine relief.

study.oneStory: At Antioch the church of the nations comes into its own. A mixed people born of grace, first called Christians, becomes the springboard for the gospel's march to the ends of the earth. The grace they received turns outward in love toward the poor of Judea, and the great story of Acts, from Jerusalem to the whole world, presses forward from this new centre.

sermon.title: The Gospel Church (Acts 11:19-30; 13:1-3)
sermon.why: Keller preaches the Antioch church directly from this passage, drawing out Barnabas's ministry of encouragement, the grace of God at work, and how a healthy gospel church grows and reaches outward, matching the heart of this study.

quiz.q: What was the significant new step taken by the believers at Antioch?

options.text: They began preaching the Lord Jesus to Greeks as well as Jews, forming a mixed church.
options.note: Verse 20 marks the deliberate turn to the Greeks, building on the Gentile breakthrough.

options.text: They preached the gospel in a large and important Gentile city.
options.note: True and part of the significance, but the key step was reaching the Greeks, not merely the location.

options.text: They began organizing the church with elders and prophets.
options.note: Prophets appear and elders are mentioned, but the defining new step was preaching to the Greeks.

options.text: They stopped preaching to Jews altogether.
options.note: They preached to Greeks as well as Jews; they did not abandon the Jews.

quiz.q: What did Barnabas see when he arrived at Antioch, and how did he respond?

options.text: He saw the grace of God, rejoiced, and encouraged them to hold fast to the Lord.
options.note: Verse 23 describes exactly this response to the evidence of grace.

options.text: He saw a large and growing crowd and was pleased at the numbers.
options.note: This mistakes mere size for the grace of God that Barnabas actually discerned.

options.text: He saw genuine faith taking root and worked to strengthen it.
options.note: Close and true, but the text specifically says he saw the grace of God and rejoiced.

options.text: He saw disorder and moved quickly to correct their errors.
options.note: He found grace to rejoice over, not error to correct.

quiz.q: Why did Barnabas go to Tarsus to find Saul?

options.text: To bring him to help teach the growing church at Antioch for a year.
options.note: Verses 25 and 26 show Barnabas fetching Saul to teach the church together.

options.text: To give the gifted Saul a role suited to his abilities.
options.note: Saul was indeed gifted, but the stated purpose was the teaching and building up of the Antioch church.

options.text: To reconcile Saul with the Jerusalem apostles.
options.note: That reconciliation had already happened earlier through Barnabas; here the aim is teaching at Antioch.

options.text: To remove Saul from ministry for a season.
options.note: The opposite is true; Barnabas drew Saul into a fruitful year of ministry.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the name Christian first being given at Antioch?

options.text: The disciples were so marked by Christ that His name became their name.
options.note: The name Christianos means those belonging to Christ, capturing the church's identity.

options.text: It gave the church a convenient label to distinguish it from Judaism.
options.note: It did distinguish them, but the deeper meaning is belonging to Christ, not mere labeling.

options.text: It showed the believers had chosen a proud title for themselves.
options.note: The name was likely given by outsiders and expresses identity in Christ, not self-promotion.

options.text: It meant the church had left behind its Jewish roots entirely.
options.note: The church remained rooted in Israel's Scriptures and Messiah; the name marks belonging to Christ, not a break from all that.

quiz.q: How did the Antioch church respond to the prophecy of a coming famine?

options.text: Each according to his ability sent relief to the brothers in Judea.
options.note: Verses 29 and 30 record this generous, organized response.

options.text: They gathered resources to protect themselves from the famine.
options.note: They gave outward to others in need rather than only securing themselves.

options.text: They sent help to fellow believers who would be affected.
options.note: True in substance, but the fuller point is that each gave according to ability, a picture of grace overflowing.

options.text: They doubted the prophecy and did nothing.
options.note: They believed Agabus and acted at once with generous relief.

questions: The believers at Antioch took the bold step of speaking about Jesus to people outside their own group. Who is outside your usual circle that God may be calling you to reach?
Barnabas rejoiced when he saw the grace of God in others. When did you last celebrate God's work in someone else's life rather than compare or compete?
Barnabas invested a year teaching new believers alongside Saul. Who is God calling you to encourage and help grow, and who has done that for you?
The disciples were first called Christians, marked out as belonging to Christ. Would those around you name you by your likeness to Jesus, and where do you long to reflect Him more?
The grace the Antioch church received overflowed into generosity toward the poor in Judea. How might the grace you have received turn outward into practical love for others in need?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered Greeks has a manuscript variant reading Hellenists, that is Greek-speaking Jews. The BSB follows the reading Greeks, meaning Gentiles, which fits Luke's contrast with verse 19 where the message went only to Jews; the footnote records the alternative.
The name Christians, Christianoi, appears only three times in the New Testament and was likely first coined by outsiders in Antioch; it means those belonging to or identified with Christ.
The phrase rendered the whole world can also mean the entire Roman world, as the BSB footnote notes; the famine under Claudius is recorded by ancient historians in the mid first century.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 11:19-30. Footnote markers and line-break objects were skipped when building verse text. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon verified this session: Tim Keller, The Gospel Church, Acts 11:19-30 and 13:1-3, on gospelinlife.com.
